title: Latin American Curanderismo and Susto de Coraje
dek: Susto de coraje is not a metaphor. It is the physical imprint of anger that was swallowed whole, trapped in the fascia and the gut, waiting for the medicine of recognition.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Anatomy of a Frightened Anger

You know the feeling. It happens at 3:14 AM when the house is quiet, but your body is loud. The masseter muscle in your jaw is locked so tight it aches into your temples. Your psoas, that deep core muscle that connects your spine to your legs, is coiled like a spring ready to snap. This is not a psychological abstraction. This is biology. This is the physical architecture of an anger that had nowhere to go.

In the traditions of Latin American curanderismo, there is a specific diagnosis for this: *susto de coraje*. It translates roughly to "fright of anger," but the English words fail to capture the somatic reality. It is the shock that occurs when a profound, righteous fury is interrupted by fear, forced back down the throat, and lodged in the tissues. The body remembers. The jaw remembers. The diaphragm, seized in a perpetual state of half-breath, remembers.

We are taught that anger is a moral failure, a lack of spiritual evolution. We are told to "let it go," to seek "love and light," to meditate it away. But the body knows better. The body knows that anger is a messenger, a fierce protector that arises when a boundary has been violated. When that protector is silenced, it does not disappear. It becomes *susto*. It becomes a ghost haunting the fascia.

Consider the smell of your own sweat when the *susto* flares up. It is not the clean, salty smell of exertion. It is sharp, acrid, the smell of cortisol and adrenaline burning through your system. Your hands might tremble. Your digestion might halt, the gut tightening into a knot that refuses to unravel. This is the physical manifestation of an interrupted defense response.


## The Wisdom of the Interrupted Response

Bessel van der Kolk, in his foundational work on trauma, articulates how the body keeps the score. He explains that when an organism is prevented from completing a defensive action—like fighting back or running away—the mobilized energy remains trapped in the nervous system. The curanderas knew this long before the language of neurobiology caught up. They understood that *susto de coraje* is exactly this: the trapped kinetic energy of an unexpressed fury.

Van der Kolk writes about the necessity of physical movement to discharge this energy. The curanderas use *barridas* (sweepings) with strong herbs like [Gaia Herbs PRO Sleep Formula](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004J32XIG?tag=spankyspinola-20) and [Solaray Total Calm Advanced](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000Z1I5EC?tag=spankyspinola-20), not just as symbolic acts, but as somatic interventions. The rough texture of the herbs against the skin, the pungent, earthy smell, the rhythmic motion—all of these serve to bring the nervous system back online, to signal to the body that the danger has passed and the frozen energy can begin to thaw.

You might feel this thawing as a sudden, violent shivering, or a wave of heat spreading across your chest. You might find yourself weeping, not from sadness, but from the sheer relief of the release. This is the body unwinding the *susto*.


## The Fascia and the Fury

Let us talk about the fascia. This continuous web of connective tissue wraps every muscle, bone, and organ in your body. It is highly innervated, rich with sensory receptors. When you swallow your anger, your fascia tightens. It creates a physical armor, a holding pattern that restricts your movement and your breath.

Resmaa Menakem, a somatic therapist and author, speaks profoundly about how trauma and unexpressed emotion live in the body, particularly in the context of systemic oppression. He emphasizes that healing must happen in the body, not just in the mind. Menakem teaches that we must learn to settle our bodies, to build the capacity to hold the charge of our emotions without dissociating or exploding.

In the context of *susto de coraje*, Menakem's work is vital. The curanderismo practice of *sobada* (massage) is a direct intervention into the fascia. The curandera's hands find the knots of trapped anger in the belly, the tight bands across the shoulders, and apply deep, sustained pressure. It is not a gentle, relaxing massage. It is a confrontation with the physical reality of the *susto*. It requires you to breathe into the pain, to stay present with the sensation of the anger being physically manipulated and released.


## The Medicine of Bitter Herbs

The medicine for *susto de coraje* is not sweet. It is bitter. In curanderismo, bitter herbs are used to stimulate the liver and the gallbladder, the organs associated with anger and resentment in many traditional medical systems, including Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM).

You might be given a tea of *ajenjo* ([Herb Pharm Stress Manager (1 oz)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005JW1F1C?tag=spankyspinola-20)) or *estafiate* (white sagebrush). The taste is shocking, astringent. It cuts through the fog of dissociation. It forces you to be present in your body. The bitterness stimulates the flow of bile, physically moving stagnation out of the digestive tract. This is not a metaphor. It is a physiological intervention designed to move the physical substrate of the trapped anger.

We are so accustomed to sweetening our medicine, to making our healing palatable. But *susto de coraje* requires a medicine that matches its intensity. It requires the fierce, uncompromising energy of the bitter herbs.


## The Somatic Practice of Reclaiming the Roar

How do we begin to unwind the *susto* in our own bodies? We start by acknowledging the physical reality of the trapped anger. We start by feeling the tension in the jaw, the tightness in the chest, the knot in the gut.

**An Embodied Practice:**

Find a quiet space where you will not be interrupted. Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent. Feel the solid ground beneath you. Bring your attention to your jaw. Notice the tension in the masseter muscles.

Slowly, deliberately, open your mouth as wide as you can. Stretch the muscles of your face. Now, take a deep breath into your belly, and as you exhale, let out a sound. It does not have to be loud at first. It can be a low groan, a sigh, a hiss.

With each breath, allow the sound to grow. Feel the vibration in your chest, in your throat. Imagine the sound originating from the deep knot in your gut, the center of the *susto*. Let the sound become a roar. Do not worry about how it sounds. Worry only about how it feels. Feel the physical release of the energy that has been trapped in your tissues.

Notice what happens in your body afterward. You might feel a tingling sensation, a rush of heat, a sudden exhaustion. This is the nervous system recalibrating. This is the body remembering how to move the energy of anger.


## The Architecture of the Throat

When anger is swallowed, the throat becomes a battleground. The muscles of the larynx and pharynx constrict, creating the physical sensation of a lump in the throat. This is the body attempting to contain the explosive energy of the unexpressed emotion. Over time, this chronic constriction can lead to physical symptoms: hoarseness, difficulty swallowing, a persistent cough.

The curanderas understand the throat as a vital energetic chokepoint. To release the *susto de coraje*, the throat must be opened. This is why the practice of vocalization, of roaring or wailing, is so crucial. It physically stretches the constricted muscles, breaking the holding pattern.

Consider the levator scapulae, the muscles that run from the back of your neck to your shoulder blades. When you are bracing against your own anger, these muscles hike your shoulders up toward your ears, a classic defensive posture. The *susto* lives here, in the chronic ache at the base of your skull. Releasing the throat often allows the shoulders to finally drop, the levator scapulae to lengthen, the physical armor to soften.


## The Digestion of Fury

The gut is the second brain, rich with neurotransmitters and deeply connected to the autonomic nervous system. When the fight-or-flight response is triggered and then interrupted, the digestive process is halted. The blood is shunted away from the stomach and intestines, preparing the muscles for action. When the action never comes, the gut remains in a state of suspended animation.

This is the physical reality of the "knot in the stomach." It is a localized ischemia, a lack of blood flow caused by chronic sympathetic nervous system arousal. The curanderismo practice of *sobada* directly addresses this. By physically massaging the abdomen, the curandera stimulates the vagus nerve, signaling the nervous system to shift from sympathetic (fight-or-flight) to parasympathetic (rest-and-digest) dominance.

The bitter herbs, too, play a role here. They stimulate the bitter receptors on the tongue, which send a reflex signal to the vagus nerve, further promoting digestive secretions and motility. The physical digestion of the herbs mirrors the energetic digestion of the anger.


## The Integration of the Shadow

We are taught to fear our anger, to see it as a destructive force. But anger, when allowed to move through the body, is a force of clarity and boundary-setting. It is the energy that says "no." It is the energy that protects our integrity.

When we heal the *susto de coraje*, we are not eliminating our anger. We are integrating it. We are reclaiming the fierce, protective energy that was trapped in our tissues. We are learning to feel the anger without being consumed by it, to let it inform our actions without dictating them.

This is the true medicine of curanderismo. It is not about transcending the body; it is about descending into it. It is about meeting the physical reality of our pain and our fury with unflinching presence and profound respect.


## The FAQ of Susto de Coraje

**Q: Is *susto de coraje* a recognized medical condition?**
**A:** In the paradigm of Western allopathic medicine, no. But in the paradigm of somatic psychology and traditional Latin American medicine, absolutely. It describes a specific physiological state of trapped sympathetic arousal resulting from interrupted anger. The symptoms—chronic muscle tension, digestive issues, anxiety—are very real and physically measurable.

**Q: Can I heal *susto de coraje* on my own, or do I need a curandera?**
**A:** While the guidance of a skilled practitioner (whether a curandera, a somatic therapist, or a bodyworker) can be invaluable, the healing ultimately happens within your own body. Practices like vocalization, shaking, and mindful attention to physical sensation can begin the process of unwinding the trapped energy. The key is to move the energy physically, not just analyze it mentally.

**Q: Why do the bitter herbs work?**
**A:** Bitter herbs stimulate the vagus nerve via receptors on the tongue, shifting the nervous system into a parasympathetic (rest-and-digest) state. They also physically stimulate the liver and gallbladder, increasing bile flow and promoting the physical digestion of stagnation. It is a physiological intervention that supports the energetic release.

**Q: I feel terrified to let my anger out. What if I lose control?**
**A:** This is the core fear of the *susto*. The key is titration—releasing the energy in small, manageable amounts. You do not have to unleash a lifetime of fury all at once. Start with a small sound, a small movement. Build your body's capacity to hold the charge of the anger without becoming overwhelmed. The goal is not an explosion, but a steady, conscious discharge.

**Q: How do I know if the *susto* is healing?**
**A:** You will feel it in your body. Your jaw will soften. Your breath will deepen, moving down into your belly instead of being trapped in your chest. Your digestion may improve. You may feel a sense of physical spaciousness where there was once only constriction. The anger will still arise when your boundaries are crossed, but it will move through you like a wave, rather than lodging in your tissues like a stone.

## The Geography of the Psoas

The psoas muscle is often called the "muscle of the soul" in certain somatic circles, but let us be more precise. It is the primary messenger of the sympathetic nervous system. It is the only muscle that connects the spine directly to the legs. When a threat is perceived, the psoas contracts instantly, preparing the body to run or to kick. When that impulse to fight back—the core of the anger response—is suppressed, the psoas remains in a state of chronic contraction.

This is where *susto de coraje* anchors itself deep in the pelvis. You might feel it as a persistent lower back ache, a tightness in the hips that no amount of stretching seems to resolve. It is not a flexibility issue; it is a nervous system issue. The psoas is holding the memory of the unexpressed kick. It is holding the boundary that was never enforced.

To release the psoas is to confront the terror of vulnerability. When the psoas finally begins to soften, the body often trembles uncontrollably. This neurogenic tremor is the nervous system discharging the trapped kinetic energy. It is the physical manifestation of the *susto* leaving the body. You must allow the trembling. You must not brace against it. It is the medicine working its way through the deep architecture of your core.


## The Alchemy of the Breath

Notice your breath right now. If you are carrying *susto de coraje*, your breath is likely shallow, trapped in the upper chest. The diaphragm, the primary muscle of respiration, is restricted. It cannot descend fully into the abdominal cavity because the gut is tight with unresolved tension.

This shallow breathing is a constant signal to the brain that danger is present. It creates a feedback loop of anxiety and hypervigilance. The curanderas know that to change the state of the nervous system, you must change the breath. But you cannot simply force a deep breath into a tight belly. You must first create the physical space for the breath to enter.

This involves physically massaging the intercostal muscles between the ribs, softening the tissue around the sternum. It involves bringing conscious awareness to the back of the ribcage, allowing the lungs to expand in three dimensions. As the breath deepens, it physically massages the vagus nerve, sending a powerful signal of safety to the brain. The *susto* cannot survive in a body that is breathing fully and deeply.


## The Lineage of the Healers

The women who practice curanderismo are not operating from a place of clinical detachment. They are operating from a lineage of embodied wisdom. They know the smell of fear and the texture of trapped anger because they have felt it in their own bodies and in the bodies of their mothers and grandmothers.

This is a medicine born of necessity, forged in the crucible of colonization and systemic oppression. The *susto de coraje* is not just an individual affliction; it is a collective one. It is the accumulated trauma of generations who were forced to swallow their fury in order to survive.

When a curandera works on your body, she is not just treating your individual symptoms. She is addressing the ancestral patterns of holding and bracing. She is calling upon the plant allies—the rue, the rosemary, the sagebrush—to assist in the heavy lifting of generational healing. It is a profound act of resistance to reclaim the body from the grip of the *susto*.


## The Physiology of the Release

What actually happens in the body when the *susto* is released? It is a cascade of physiological events. The sympathetic nervous system downregulates, reducing the production of cortisol and adrenaline. The parasympathetic nervous system comes online, slowing the heart rate and increasing digestive motility.

The fascia, which has been held in a state of rigid contraction, begins to rehydrate and soften. The ground substance of the fascia, which can become thick and gel-like under stress, becomes more fluid, allowing for greater glide between the muscle fibers. The localized ischemia in the gut resolves as blood flow returns to the digestive organs.

You might experience this as a sudden wave of exhaustion, a deep, bone-weary tiredness. This is the body finally allowing itself to rest after months or years of chronic bracing. It is essential to honor this exhaustion, to allow the body the time and space it needs to integrate the profound physiological shifts that have occurred.


## The Ritual of the Return

Healing *susto de coraje* is not a one-time event. It is a process of returning, again and again, to the physical reality of the body. It is a commitment to feeling the sensations that arise, even when they are uncomfortable or terrifying.

The curanderas often prescribe specific rituals to support this process of return. These might involve spiritual baths with specific herbs, the burning of copal or sage, or the creation of an altar to honor the protective energy of the anger. These rituals are not empty superstitions; they are somatic anchors. They provide a structured container for the nervous system to process the intense energies of the release.

The ritual of the return is also the daily practice of checking in with the body. It is the practice of noticing when the jaw begins to tighten, when the breath becomes shallow, and consciously choosing to soften, to breathe, to move the energy before it becomes trapped again. It is the ongoing work of reclaiming the body as a safe and sovereign space.


## The Sovereignty of the Flesh

Ultimately, the healing of *susto de coraje* is about reclaiming the sovereignty of the flesh. It is about refusing to allow the unexpressed anger to dictate the terms of your physical existence. It is about taking back the territory of your own body.

This is not a gentle process. It requires courage, the willingness to face the fierce and uncompromising reality of your own internal landscape. It requires the willingness to roar, to tremble, to weep. But the reward is profound. It is the return of your vitality, your clarity, your capacity for deep and authentic connection.

When the *susto* is cleared, the body becomes a resonant chamber for the truth. The anger is no longer a ghost haunting the fascia; it is a powerful ally, a clear and ringing bell that signals when a boundary has been crossed. This is the true medicine. This is the wisdom of the body, restored to its rightful place.
